meta.title: CTL / LTL & Automata — Practice
meta.note: CTL verdicts were computed by a fair-CTL model checker; each existence claim carries a verified witness path/lasso. Buchi and expressibility items are from a hand-verified bank.

questions.prompt: Let p, q be Boolean atoms. Does there exist a CTL formula expressing the same property as the LTL formula  GFq ?
questions.explanation: Yes — "infinitely often q" is expressible: GFq is equivalent to the CTL formula AGAFq.

questions.prompt:
Consider the fair Kripke model M below.
Is   M ⊨ EG p   true or false?

questions.explanation: True — the fair path (s0 → s2)ω keeps p true forever.

questions.prompt:
Consider the Kripke model M below.
Is   M ⊨ E(q U p)   true or false?

questions.explanation: True — along s0 → s2, q holds until p becomes true at s2.

questions.prompt: Let p, q be Boolean atoms. Does there exist a CTL formula expressing the same property as the LTL formula  Fp ?
questions.explanation: Yes — Fp is equivalent to the CTL formula AFp.

questions.prompt: Let p, q be Boolean atoms. Does there exist a CTL formula expressing the same property as the LTL formula  GFp ?
questions.explanation: Yes — GFp ("infinitely often p") is equivalent to the CTL formula AGAFp.

questions.prompt: Let p, q be Boolean atoms. Does there exist a CTL formula expressing the same property as the LTL formula  FGp ?
questions.explanation: No — FGp has no equivalent CTL formula (AFAGp is not equivalent to it).

questions.prompt:
The Büchi automaton below is claimed to recognize the language of the LTL formula  qUp .
Is this claim correct?
questions.diagramSvg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 170" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="ui-monospace,Menlo,Consolas,monospace" font-size="14"><defs><marker id="ah" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="7.5" refY="3" orient="auto" markerUnits="strokeWidth"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#333"/></marker></defs><path d="M102,64 C78,26 142,26 118,64" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#ah)"/><text x="110" y="30" text-anchor="middle">q</text><path d="M136.0,90.0 Q175.0,116.0 214.0,90.0" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#ah)"/><text x="175.0" y="112.0" text-anchor="middle">p</text><path d="M232,64 C208,26 272,26 248,64" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#ah)"/><text x="240" y="30" text-anchor="middle">⊤</text><path d="M52,90 L82,90" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#ah)"/><circle cx="110" cy="90" r="24" fill="#fff" stroke="#222" stroke-width="2"/><text x="110" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">A</text><circle cx="240" cy="90" r="24" fill="#fff" stroke="#222" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="240" cy="90" r="19" fill="none" stroke="#222" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="240" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">B</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Yes — the initial state loops only on q and exits to the accepting sink on p, so acceptance ⇔ q holds up to the first p = qUp.

questions.prompt: Let p, q be Boolean atoms. Does there exist a CTL formula expressing the same property as the LTL formula  Fq ?
questions.explanation: Yes — Fq is equivalent to the CTL formula AFq.
